questions.questionText: The most common cause of hyperthyroidism (thyrotoxicosis) is:
questions.explanation: Graves' disease is an autoimmune condition caused by stimulating TSI antibodies.

questions.questionText: Hashimoto thyroiditis is histologically characterized by:
questions.optionB: Extensive lymphocytic infiltrate with germinal centers and Hurthle cells
questions.optionC: Papillary projections and Orphan Annie eye nuclei
questions.explanation: The destruction of the thyroid gland is marked by prominent lymphoid aggregates.

questions.questionText: The most common type of thyroid carcinoma is:
questions.explanation: Papillary carcinoma accounts for about 80% of all thyroid cancers and has an excellent prognosis.

questions.questionText: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from which cell type?
questions.explanation: Medullary carcinomas secrete calcitonin and may present as part of MEN 2 syndromes.

questions.questionText: Primary hyperparathyroidism is most often caused by a:
questions.explanation: A solitary benign parathyroid adenoma is responsible for >80% of cases.

questions.questionText: Cushing syndrome caused specifically by an ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma is called:
questions.explanation: Cushing *disease* specifically refers to a pituitary origin for the hypercortisolism.

questions.questionText: Primary chronic adrenocortical insufficiency is known as:
questions.explanation: Addison disease involves autoimmune destruction of the entire adrenal cortex.

questions.questionText: Pheochromocytomas are tumors of the adrenal medulla that secrete:
questions.explanation: They cause episodic hypertension, palpitations, and sweating.

questions.questionText: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus is primarily caused by:
questions.optionB: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
questions.explanation: Absolute insulin deficiency occurs due to T-cell mediated destruction of beta cells.

questions.questionText: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 1 (MEN 1 - Wermer syndrome) involves tumors of the '3 Ps':
questions.explanation: These are the classic sites for tumors in the MEN 1 syndrome.

questions.questionText: In Endocrine Pathology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Endocrine Pathology. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Endocrine Pathology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Endocrine Pathology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Endocrine Pathology.
